prompt: The ring buffer implementation corrupts data when it wraps around. Reading after a wraparound returns garbage data. Find the off-by-one error.

turns.content:
<task>
The ring buffer implementation corrupts data when it wraps around. Reading after a wraparound returns garbage data. Find the off-by-one error.
</task>

arguments.reasoning: Found the off-by-one error. Lines 26 and 38 use `% (rb->capacity + 1)` for the modulo operation. It should be `% rb->capacity`. With capacity=8 and the +1, the modulo is 9, so head/tail can reach position 8 (index 8 in a buffer of size 8, which is out of bounds). This causes writing/reading one byte past the allocated buffer, and the wrap-around happens at the wrong point, misaligning subsequent reads.

arguments.summary: Found off-by-one in src/ringbuf.c. Lines 26 and 38 both use `% (rb->capacity + 1)` instead of `% rb->capacity`. With capacity=8, modulo 9 means head/tail reach index 8 (out of bounds for 8-byte buffer). Also causes wrap-around at wrong position — head and tail get out of sync with actual buffer, corrupting data after wraparound.

turns.content:
gcc -Wall -Wextra -o test_ringbuf tests/test_ringbuf.c src/ringbuf.c
./test_ringbuf
Test basic write/read: PASS
Test wraparound: PASS
Test full buffer: PASS
Test overflow rejected: PASS
All tests passed!

arguments.summary: All 4 tests pass including 'Test wraparound' which was previously failing. Fixed both modulo operations from `% (capacity + 1)` to `% capacity`.
arguments.final_answer:
Fixed ring buffer off-by-one error in src/ringbuf.c. Both ringbuf_write (line 26) and ringbuf_read (line 38) used `% (rb->capacity + 1)` instead of `% rb->capacity` for the wrap-around modulo. This caused:
1. Out-of-bounds access at index `capacity` (one past the buffer)
2. Incorrect wrap-around point, misaligning head and tail pointers after the first wrap
Fixed by removing the `+ 1` from both modulo operations. All 4 tests pass.
